enrichment.short_description: edX is an online learning marketplace offering courses, professional certificates, and degree programs from universities and companies worldwide, with free auditing and paid verified credentials.
enrichment.company_size: Large (owned by 2U, Inc., a private company)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: edX offers university-backed online courses and certificates. See 2026 pricing, free audit options, MicroMasters costs, features, and how it compares to Coursera.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
edX is an online learning platform that hosts massive open online courses (MOOCs), professional certificates, MicroMasters programs, and fully online degrees created in partnership with universities and companies such as Harvard, MIT, Columbia, IBM, and Google. It was founded in 2012 by Harvard University and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology as a joint nonprofit venture, with MIT's Anant Agarwal serving as founding CEO and MIT's Rafael Reif and researcher Piotr Mitros credited among its architects. The founding mission was to democratize access to elite university-level education by putting real course content from top institutions online, mostly for free, while also using the platform as a research tool for studying how people learn. In its first decade edX grew into one of the largest MOOC providers alongside Coursera and the now largely defunct Udacity, hosting thousands of courses across computer science, data science, business, engineering, humanities, and public health.

The nonprofit era ended in 2021, when edX's board sold the platform to 2U, Inc., a publicly traded online program management company, for $800 million in cash. The sale proceeds were used to establish a separate nonprofit, Axim Collaborative, to continue edX's original public-interest mission, while the edX brand, technology, and course catalog became a commercial 2U product. The acquisition proved financially troubled for 2U: burdened by debt and a slowing online-degree market, 2U filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in July 2024. The company emerged from bankruptcy later that year as a private business, eliminating more than $450 million in debt, and as of mid-2026 continues to operate edX as part of its portfolio alongside its other online-education services.

Technically, edX runs on the Open edX platform, an open-source learning management system originally built by edX itself and now maintained by a broader community of institutions and vendors under the Axim Collaborative and Open edX contributor ecosystem. Open edX is used not only to power edx.org but also by many universities and companies to run their own independent learning portals. On edx.org, learners browse a searchable catalog, watch video lectures, complete auto-graded and peer-graded assignments, participate in discussion forums, and track progress through a personal dashboard. Course content is produced by the partner university or company, not by edX itself, so quality and format vary by provider, though edX applies its own instructional-design standards during course review.

edX's pricing structure is transactional rather than subscription-based, which distinguishes it from Coursera Plus. Most courses can be audited entirely free, which unlocks video lectures and, in many cases, reading materials, but not graded assignments or a certificate. To receive a verified certificate carrying the institution's name and a shareable credential, learners pay per course, typically ranging from $50 to $300 depending on the subject and issuing institution. Stackable credentials cost more: Professional Certificate programs (multi-course sequences) generally run from about $299 to $1,500 or more, and MicroMasters programs, which can carry credit toward a full master's degree at partner universities, typically cost $600 to $1,500 for the full program. A smaller number of programs offer bundled annual access, with subscription-style options priced around $199 per year for unlimited certificates within a specific track. Since the 2U acquisition, some free-audit options have been scaled back on certain courses, and pricing has trended upward compared to edX's nonprofit years, a shift that has drawn criticism from higher-education commentators.

edX's target users span self-directed lifelong learners upgrading job skills, university students supplementing formal coursework, career changers pursuing professional certificates in fields like data analytics or cybersecurity, and organizations sourcing corporate training content. Its main competitors are Coursera, which offers a comparable university-partner model plus a flat-rate subscription; Udemy, which focuses on instructor-created courses at low fixed prices; LinkedIn Learning, aimed at corporate upskilling; and Khan Academy and FutureLearn for free and university-partnered learning respectively. edX differentiates itself through direct branding from prestigious universities on certificates, its open-source Open edX platform that other institutions can self-host, and deep integration with formal credit-bearing programs such as MicroMasters and MicroBachelors. Notable facts include edX's origins as a genuinely nonprofit, open-source project (a rarity among major MOOC platforms), its $800 million sale that remains one of the largest transactions in ed-tech history, and its status today as a for-profit product operating under a financially restructured parent company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: edX is an online learning marketplace built around courses and credentials created by real universities and companies rather than independent instructors. Learners can audit thousands of courses for free or pay for a verified certificate, professional certificate program, or MicroMasters credential that can sometimes count toward a full degree.

Originally founded in 2012 as a nonprofit joint venture between Harvard and MIT, edX was acquired by 2U, Inc. in 2021 for $800 million and now operates as a commercial product. The underlying Open edX software remains open source and is used independently by many institutions to run their own learning platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers self-paced and instructor-paced courses with video lectures, auto-graded quizzes, peer-graded assignments, and discussion forums. A personal dashboard tracks enrolled courses, deadlines, and earned certificates across a learner's full history on the platform.

Beyond individual courses, edX structures learning into stackable pathways: Professional Certificates for job-focused skill sequences, MicroMasters programs that can articulate into full master's degrees at partner universities, and MicroBachelors programs for undergraduate-level credit. Corporate and campus editions let organizations license the Open edX platform or curated content for internal training.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: edX does not sell a flat monthly subscription for most content; instead, learners audit courses for free and pay per credential. Verified certificates for individual courses typically cost between $50 and $300, while Professional Certificate programs range roughly from $299 to $1,500 or more depending on length and institution.

MicroMasters programs, which bundle graduate-level coursework, generally cost between $600 and $1,500 for the complete sequence. A limited number of programs offer bundled annual access for around $199 per year. Since the 2U acquisition, free-audit access has been reduced on some courses and prices have trended higher than in edX's nonprofit era.

faqs.question: Is edX free to use?
faqs.answer: Auditing most courses is free, which includes video lectures and often reading materials. A verified certificate, graded assignments, or credit-bearing programs require payment.

faqs.question: How much does an edX verified certificate cost?
faqs.answer: Verified certificates for individual courses typically range from $50 to $300 depending on the course and the issuing institution.

faqs.question: Who owns edX?
faqs.answer: edX was founded as a nonprofit by Harvard and MIT in 2012 and was acquired by 2U, Inc. for $800 million in 2021. It remains part of 2U, which restructured through Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 and continues operating as a private company.

faqs.question: What is a MicroMasters program?
faqs.answer: A MicroMasters is a graduate-level sequence of courses from a specific university that can be completed for a credential and, in many cases, counted toward credit if the learner is later admitted to that university's full master's degree.

faqs.question: Does edX offer full degrees?
faqs.answer: Yes, edX partners with universities to offer select fully online bachelor's and master's degree programs, in addition to shorter certificate and MicroMasters credentials.

faqs.question: How does edX compare to Coursera?
faqs.answer: Both platforms partner with universities and companies, but Coursera offers a flat-rate Coursera Plus subscription for broad access, while edX generally charges per course or per credential rather than a single subscription.

enrichment.short_description: EGroupware is an open-source groupware suite for email, calendaring, contacts, file sharing, and project management, available as free self-hosted software or paid cloud and enterprise editions.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); paid cloud plans from EUR 21.90/month
enrichment.seo_meta_description: EGroupware is an open-source groupware platform for email, calendar, files, and collaboration. Compare Community Edition, cloud, and enterprise pricing for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
EGroupware is an open-source groupware and collaboration suite that combines email, shared calendars, contact management, task and project tracking, file storage, and team chat into a single self-hostable platform. It is positioned as an open-source alternative to Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace for organizations, particularly in Europe, that want to keep collaboration data under their own control rather than in a large US-based cloud. The software traces its roots to 1996, when the underlying groupware concept was first developed to support a German personnel-training agency's internal workflows. In 2003 the project was forked from the earlier phpGroupWare codebase and relaunched under the GPL as EGroupware, establishing it as an independent open-source project with a growing community of contributors. Commercial stewardship of the project passed through Stylite AG before EGroupware GmbH, based in Kaiserslautern, Germany, took over the business division in September 2016 and has run the project since.

Technically, EGroupware is a PHP-based web application backed by a MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL database, deployed either on a customer's own servers (on-premises) or through EGroupware's own cloud hosting in German data centers. Its modular architecture bundles core applications including Mail, Calendar, Addressbook, InfoLog (task and CRM-style tracking), Filemanager, Projectmanager, and a helpdesk/ticketing module, and it can be extended with third-party or custom apps. Real-time document editing is handled through an integration with Collabora Online, and video conferencing is supported via bundled Jitsi or BigBlueButton integrations. Mobile users sync calendars, contacts, and tasks with native Apple and Android device calendars and address books using standard CalDAV and CardDAV protocols, and a dedicated mobile app is also available.

EGroupware is distributed under two license tracks. The Community Edition (CE) is released under the GPLv2 and contains the full core functionality with no artificial limitations, making it free to download, self-host, and modify indefinitely. The Enterprise Professional Line (EPL) adds features aimed at larger organizations, such as additional applications, more advanced administration tools, and access to paid vendor support, and is licensed per named or concurrent user on an annual basis. Organizations that do not want to manage their own servers can instead subscribe to EGroupware's hosted cloud plans.

Pricing for the cloud plans starts at eCloud NGO at EUR 21.90 per month for nonprofit organizations and eCloud at EUR 24.90 per month for standard commercial use, both requiring a minimum of two concurrent users, with Collabora document editing and Rocket.Chat team messaging available as metered add-ons. A flat-rate eFlat School plan is available at EUR 1,295 per year for unlimited users, aimed at educational institutions, while the top-tier eFlat plan for larger organizations is quoted on request. For self-hosted deployments, the on-premises EPL Smart package starts at EUR 62.50 per month for a minimum of ten concurrent users, with additional users billed at EUR 4.95 per month each, while the top Universal tier is custom-priced for large-scale or highly customized rollouts. Support for the free Community Edition is available separately at an hourly consulting rate. All published prices exclude VAT.

EGroupware's target users are small and mid-sized businesses, public-sector bodies, schools, and nonprofits, particularly in Germany and the broader European Union, that need a full collaboration suite but prefer open-source software, data sovereignty, or lower long-term licensing costs compared to proprietary alternatives. Its closest competitors include Nextcloud (which focuses more on file sync and sharing with added collaboration apps), Zimbra Collaboration, and the proprietary incumbents Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace. EGroupware differentiates itself by offering a genuinely free, fully-featured GPL edition alongside a clear enterprise upgrade path, German-hosted cloud infrastructure for data-residency-conscious customers, and native groupware features like InfoLog and integrated helpdesk ticketing that go beyond simple file sharing. Notable facts include its two-decade-plus project history, its GPLv2 licensing that predates most modern open-source collaboration suites, and its continued operation as an independently owned German software company rather than a venture-backed startup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EGroupware is an open-source groupware suite that bundles email, calendaring, contacts, file storage, project tracking, and team chat into one collaboration platform. It can be self-hosted for free under the GPLv2 or run as a paid, German-hosted cloud service.

The project traces back to 1996 and was relaunched as an independent GPL fork of phpGroupWare in 2003. EGroupware GmbH, headquartered in Kaiserslautern, Germany, has operated the commercial side of the project since taking over from Stylite AG in 2016.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core modules cover shared calendars, an address book, InfoLog for task and lightweight CRM tracking, a file manager, and a project manager, all accessible from one web interface and synced to mobile devices via CalDAV and CardDAV. Real-time collaborative document editing is available through a Collabora Online integration, and video calls run through bundled Jitsi or BigBlueButton connectors.

The platform is modular, letting administrators enable only the applications a team needs, and it supports team chat through Rocket.Chat integration. A helpdesk and ticketing module is also available for organizations that want to manage internal or customer support requests inside the same system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and unlimited under the GPLv2, with paid hourly support available separately. Hosted cloud plans start at EUR 21.90 per month for nonprofits (eCloud NGO) and EUR 24.90 per month for standard use (eCloud), both with a two-user minimum, plus metered add-ons for Collabora and Rocket.Chat.

Self-hosted enterprise customers can license EPL Smart from EUR 62.50 per month for a ten-user minimum, with additional users at EUR 4.95 per month, while schools can choose a flat EUR 1,295 per year unlimited-user plan. The largest Universal and eFlat tiers are custom-quoted. All prices exclude VAT.

faqs.question: Is EGroupware really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community Edition is released under the GPLv2 and can be downloaded and self-hosted at no cost, with the full core feature set and no artificial usage limits.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Community Edition and EPL?
faqs.answer: Community Edition (CE) is the free, open-source core. The Enterprise Professional Line (EPL) adds extra applications, administration tools, and paid vendor support, licensed per user on an annual or monthly basis.

faqs.question: Where is EGroupware hosted for cloud customers?
faqs.answer: EGroupware's cloud plans, such as eCloud and eCloud NGO, are hosted in German data centers, which appeals to organizations with EU data-residency requirements.

faqs.question: Can EGroupware sync with my phone's calendar and contacts?
faqs.answer: Yes, EGroupware supports CalDAV and CardDAV synchronization with Apple and Android devices, and also offers dedicated mobile apps.

faqs.question: Does EGroupware support real-time document editing?
faqs.answer: Yes, through an integration with Collabora Online, which enables collaborative editing of office documents directly in the browser.

faqs.question: Who is behind EGroupware?
faqs.answer: EGroupware GmbH, based in Kaiserslautern, Germany, has developed and commercially supported the open-source EGroupware project since taking over the business from Stylite AG in September 2016.

enrichment.short_description: ejabberd is an open-source, Erlang-based XMPP, MQTT, and SIP server known for massive scalability, used to power large real-time messaging systems including early WhatsApp-style infrastructure and Nintendo Switch's chat network.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ejabberd is an open-source XMPP, MQTT, and SIP server built for massive scale. See 2026 features, Community vs Business Edition pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
ejabberd is an open-source, real-time messaging server that implements the XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) standard along with MQTT for IoT messaging and SIP for voice signaling. Written in Erlang, a programming language originally built by Ericsson for fault-tolerant telecom systems, ejabberd is designed from the ground up for horizontal scalability, high availability, and clustering across multiple servers. The project was started in 2002 by Russian developer Alexey Shchepin as one of the earliest fully XMPP-compliant open-source servers. In 2005, the French company ProcessOne was founded specifically to take over long-term maintenance, commercialization, and enterprise support of ejabberd, and it has remained the project's primary steward ever since, employing much of the original core engineering team.

ejabberd's technical architecture leverages Erlang/OTP's lightweight process model and built-in distribution capabilities to support clustering, allowing a single node to handle roughly a million to two million concurrently connected users and clusters to scale to tens of millions. Its module system covers the full range of XMPP extension protocols (XEPs), including multi-user chat rooms, publish-subscribe, message archive management, and push notifications, and it can be extended through custom Erlang or Lua modules. Beyond XMPP, ejabberd bundles native MQTT broker support for IoT device messaging and SIP proxy capabilities for voice, making it usable as a general-purpose real-time communication backend rather than a chat-only server. It stores data in Mnesia (Erlang's built-in database) by default but also supports external SQL databases such as PostgreSQL and MySQL for larger deployments.

ejabberd is most famous for its role in the history of consumer messaging: WhatsApp's early server architecture was heavily influenced by and reportedly built on a modified version of ejabberd, and in 2009 researchers detected that Facebook's chat.facebook.com service was running a modified ejabberd instance. More recently, ejabberd has been used to power the friend-list and communication backend of the Nintendo Switch and has been adopted by the BBC and various telecom and IoT companies for large-scale real-time messaging infrastructure. This track record of powering some of the highest-concurrency chat systems in the world is central to ejabberd's reputation as one of the most battle-tested XMPP servers available.

ejabberd is distributed under a dual structure. The Community Edition is fully open source under the GPLv2, freely downloadable from GitHub, and usable in production without licensing fees, though it comes without a formal support contract. The Business Edition adds enterprise-oriented features such as enhanced performance modules for geo-distributed deployments, expanded mobile push capabilities, Long-Term Support (LTS) releases maintained for extended periods, and direct service-level agreements with ProcessOne's engineering team, including options for 24/7 support. ProcessOne also offers Fluux, a fully managed, ejabberd-based messaging cloud service for teams that do not want to operate their own servers. Pricing for both the Business Edition and Fluux is not publicly listed and requires contacting ProcessOne directly, following a common pattern among open-core infrastructure vendors that price based on scale, support tier, and deployment complexity.

ejabberd's target users are engineering teams building chat, presence, or real-time notification features into consumer apps, IoT platforms needing a reliable MQTT broker, and large organizations such as telecoms, gaming companies, and public broadcasters that need a proven, horizontally scalable messaging backbone. Its closest open-source competitors include Openfire (Java-based, simpler to operate but less proven at extreme scale), Prosody (lightweight, Lua-based XMPP server popular for smaller deployments), Tigase, and MongooseIM (also Erlang-based). It also competes indirectly with the newer Matrix protocol ecosystem, whose reference server Synapse and client Element offer end-to-end encryption and federation as core design principles rather than as XMPP extensions. ejabberd differentiates itself through its multi-protocol support (XMPP, MQTT, and SIP in one server), its Erlang-based fault tolerance, and an unusually long and well-documented history of running some of the largest real-time messaging deployments in the world.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ejabberd is an open-source, Erlang-based server that implements the XMPP messaging protocol along with MQTT and SIP support, making it a flexible backend for chat, presence, IoT messaging, and voice signaling. It was started in 2002 by Alexey Shchepin and has been maintained since 2005 by ProcessOne, a French company founded specifically to support the project commercially.

The server is best known for its role in real-time messaging infrastructure at massive scale, having influenced WhatsApp's early architecture, powered a modified deployment behind Facebook's original chat feature, and today running the Nintendo Switch friend and communication network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ejabberd supports the full range of XMPP extension protocols including multi-user chat rooms, publish-subscribe messaging, message archive management, and mobile push notifications, all built on Erlang/OTP's distributed, fault-tolerant runtime. Clustering allows a single node to handle roughly a million to two million concurrent connections, with horizontal scaling across nodes for larger deployments.

Beyond chat, ejabberd includes a native MQTT broker for IoT device messaging and a SIP proxy for voice signaling, letting a single server handle multiple real-time communication protocols. It can be extended with custom Erlang or Lua modules and integrates with external SQL databases for large-scale data storage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and open source under the GPLv2, with full production functionality and no licensing fees, though it does not include a formal support contract from ProcessOne.

The Business Edition adds enterprise features like Long-Term Support releases, enhanced performance modules, and direct SLAs with 24/7 support options, while Fluux offers a fully managed, hosted ejabberd cloud. Both are custom-priced and require contacting ProcessOne directly for a quote.

faqs.question: Who maintains ejabberd?

enrichment.short_description: eLabFTW is a free, open-source electronic lab notebook and inventory manager for research labs, self-hostable at no cost, with optional paid support and hosting from its maker, Deltablot.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: eLabFTW is a free, open-source electronic lab notebook for research teams. See 2026 self-hosting details, PRO support and hosting pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
eLabFTW is a free and open-source electronic laboratory notebook (ELN) built for experimental research teams that need to document protocols, track samples and reagents, and maintain an auditable, timestamped record of lab work. It was created by Nicolas Carpi, a research engineer at the Institut Curie in Paris, who first released the software around 2012 after finding that no adequate electronic notebook existed for his own day-to-day lab workflow. What began as a personal side project grew steadily in adoption across academic and research institutions, and by 2019 the project's popularity led Carpi to found Deltablot SASU, a company based in Paris, France, dedicated to supporting eLabFTW's ongoing development, professional support, and managed hosting.

eLabFTW is a web application written in PHP, released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL), which guarantees that the source code and any modifications remain free and open even when the software is run as a network service. It is typically deployed via Docker, making self-hosted installation straightforward for institutional IT teams, and stores experiment data, file attachments, and metadata in a structured database that supports full version history and immutable timestamping of locked entries. The platform combines two core modules: an experiments module for documenting protocols, results, and observations in a rich-text, timestampable notebook, and a database/inventory module for cataloging samples, reagents, antibodies, cell lines, equipment, and other lab resources with custom metadata fields. Entries can be linked to each other, tagged, exported, and shared with collaborators, and the platform supports electronic signatures and audit logging to help labs meet reproducibility and compliance requirements such as those found in ISO 17025 or good laboratory practice environments.

Because eLabFTW is fully open source, there are no features locked behind a paywall: any lab with the technical capacity to self-host the software gets the complete feature set for free. Deltablot instead monetizes optional professional services layered on top of the free software. A PRO Support subscription, priced from EUR 760 per year, gives self-hosting institutions direct access to Deltablot's engineering team for troubleshooting, upgrades, and configuration help. For labs that prefer not to manage their own servers, Deltablot offers PRO Hosting, a fully managed cloud version of eLabFTW with automated backups, a custom domain, and infrastructure hosted in Europe, the United States, or Asia depending on customer preference, priced according to lab size and requirements. At the top of the range, PRO Hosting SecNumCloud, priced at EUR 6,985 per year, is a compliance-grade hosting tier built for organizations with strict data-sovereignty and security requirements, hosted exclusively in France by SecNumCloud-qualified providers and including encryption at rest, HDS health-data certification eligibility, and ISO 27001-aligned infrastructure.

eLabFTW's target users are academic research groups, university core facilities, biotech and pharmaceutical R&D teams, and government research institutions that need rigorous, auditable documentation of experimental work, often as a requirement for grant compliance, publication reproducibility, or regulatory audits. It has been adopted by numerous European universities and research computing centers, including deployments referenced by institutions such as Heidelberg University's computing center and the University of Graz, reflecting particularly strong adoption in academic settings across Germany, France, and Austria. Its main competitors include commercial electronic lab notebook platforms such as Benchling, LabArchives, RSpace, and SciNote, most of which are proprietary SaaS products with per-seat subscription pricing. eLabFTW differentiates itself as one of the only ELNs that is fully open source and free to self-host with zero feature restrictions, appealing especially to institutions with strong open-science principles, tight budgets, or strict requirements to keep experimental data on infrastructure they control. Notable facts include its academic origin story at a major cancer research institute, its AGPL licensing (a relatively strict open-source license chosen specifically to prevent closed-source forks from being offered as a service), and its growth from a single researcher's side project into a dedicated company serving research labs across Europe, North America, and beyond.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: eLabFTW is a free, open-source electronic lab notebook that lets research teams document experiments, track samples and reagents, and keep a timestamped, auditable record of lab work. It was created by Nicolas Carpi at the Institut Curie in Paris and first released around 2012.

The project grew from a personal tool into one of the most widely used open-source ELNs in academic research, leading Carpi to found Deltablot SASU in 2019 to provide professional support and managed hosting around the free software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform combines an experiments module, where researchers write rich-text protocols and results that can be locked and timestamped for reproducibility, with a database/inventory module for cataloging samples, reagents, and equipment using custom metadata fields. Entries can be cross-linked, tagged, and exported for sharing or archival.

eLabFTW supports electronic signatures, detailed audit logs, and version history to help labs meet compliance needs such as ISO 17025 or good laboratory practice. It is deployed via Docker for straightforward self-hosting and is released under the AGPL, ensuring the code stays open even when run as a hosted service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core software is completely free with no features behind a paywall; any lab that self-hosts eLabFTW gets the full feature set at no cost under the AGPL license.

Deltablot monetizes optional services: PRO Support starts from EUR 760 per year for direct engineering help with self-hosted instances, PRO Hosting offers fully managed cloud deployment in Europe, the US, or Asia at custom pricing, and PRO Hosting SecNumCloud, at EUR 6,985 per year, provides compliance-grade hosting exclusively in France for organizations with strict data-sovereignty needs.

enrichment.seo_title: Elai.io Review 2026: AI Video Generator Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Elai.io turns text into AI avatar videos for training and e-learning. See 2026 pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares to Synthesia.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Elai is an AI video generation platform that converts written scripts, slides, or documents into presenter-led videos featuring AI avatars and synthetic voices, without requiring cameras, actors, or traditional video production. It was co-founded in 2021 by Vitalii Romanchenko and Alex Uspenskyi, and the flagship Elai Builder product launched in 2022, where it was recognized as the number one Product of the Day on Product Hunt shortly after release. The platform grew quickly by focusing on a specific niche within the broader AI video category: corporate training, onboarding, and e-learning content, rather than trying to serve marketing and social video creators as its primary audience. By the time of its acquisition, Elai had built a customer base of more than 2,000 organizations, including large enterprises such as Fujitsu, Beiersdorf, and Generali. In late 2024, Elai was acquired by Panopto, a video platform company best known for lecture capture and enterprise video management software used widely in higher education and corporate learning and development, positioning Elai's AI avatar generation as a complementary capability inside Panopto's broader video ecosystem.

Technically, Elai works by having users either write or paste a script, or upload an existing document or PowerPoint presentation, which the platform's AI then converts into a video featuring a chosen AI avatar reading the content aloud in a synthesized voice. Users can customize slides with their own branding, images, and templates, and the tool automatically generates avatar lip-sync and gestures matched to the spoken script. A distinguishing feature of Elai's platform is its support for interactive video elements embedded directly in the player: branching paths based on viewer choices, clickable buttons, hotspots, text-based quizzes, and image-based quizzes, which let training teams build assessment and engagement directly into a video rather than bolting it onto a separate LMS quiz. The platform supports 80 or more diverse AI avatars and more than 75 languages with over 450 regional accents, making it well suited for multinational organizations that need to localize training content at scale without re-shooting video.

Elai's pricing is structured around monthly video-minute budgets rather than a flat seat fee. The Free plan provides one minute of video generation with no credit card required, giving new users a genuine hands-on trial of the avatar and language library before committing. The Creator plan costs $29 per month billed monthly, or roughly $23 per month billed annually (about $276 per year), and includes 15 minutes of video generation per month in full HD with access to the complete avatar, voice, and language library. The Team plan, Elai's most popular tier, costs $125 per month billed monthly or about $100 per month billed annually ($1,200 per year), and includes 50 minutes of video per month for three editors and three guest collaborators, along with 4K Ultra HD export, 100 or more premium voices, custom image and font uploads, custom background music, custom templates, workspace collaboration tools, image-based quizzes, and automatic translation; additional minute allotments (100 or 150 minutes per month) are also available at this tier. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and offers unlimited users and custom minute allocations, three included voice clones, three included selfie avatars, premium avatar access, brand kit tools, single sign-on (SSO), SCORM export for LMS compatibility, direct Panopto integration, SOC 2 compliance, dedicated onboarding and product training, and premium support. Additional a la carte add-ons are available across plans, including a custom Studio Avatar for $500 per year, a personal Selfie Avatar for $199 per year, voice cloning for $200 per year, and extra video minutes at $2 per minute on paid plans.

Elai's target users are corporate learning and development teams, HR and onboarding departments, internal communications teams, and e-learning content creators who need to produce large volumes of instructional video without traditional filming, translation, or voiceover costs. Its most direct competitors are Synthesia, the market leader in AI avatar video generation and the platform Elai is most frequently compared against; HeyGen, known for fast-turnaround marketing and social-style avatar videos; Colossyan, which also targets corporate learning specifically; and D-ID, known for its underlying avatar animation technology. Elai differentiates itself through its deep focus on interactive, quiz-enabled training video and SCORM/LMS export, its acquisition-driven integration with Panopto's enterprise video infrastructure, and pricing that is generally positioned below Synthesia's comparable tiers for teams with moderate monthly video-minute needs. Notable facts include its rapid rise as a Product Hunt-recognized launch, its enterprise customer roster spanning global brands like Fujitsu and Generali, and its 2024 acquisition by Panopto, which folded a five-year-old AI video startup into a much larger, established enterprise video company.

enrichment.short_description: Elastic Email is a cloud-based email delivery platform combining a transactional SMTP relay and REST API with email marketing tools, letting developers and marketers send, track, and optimize high-volume email from a single dashboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (pay-as-you-go credits plus tiered monthly subscriptions)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid Email API plans from $19/month; Email Marketing plans from $29/month)
enrichment.headquarters: Vancouver, Canada (with a second office in Szczecin, Poland)
enrichment.seo_title: Elastic Email Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Elastic Email review 2026: pricing, SMTP and API features, deliverability tools, and how it compares to SendGrid, Mailgun, and Postmark for developers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Elastic Email is a cloud-based email delivery and marketing platform built around two closely integrated products: a high-volume transactional email API and SMTP relay for developers, and a browser-based email marketing suite for campaign creators. The platform lets technical teams send account notifications, receipts, password resets, and other system-triggered messages through REST API calls or standard SMTP, while marketers can build newsletters and automated drip sequences using a drag-and-drop email designer, list segmentation, and analytics dashboards. Because both products share the same sending infrastructure, IP reputation pools, and account, customers can move between transactional and bulk marketing sends without adopting a second vendor, which is one of the platform's core differentiators against tools that specialize in only one of those two use cases.

The company was founded in 2010 by a small team of Polish and Canadian engineers who set out to build a more affordable, developer-friendly alternative to the enterprise email service providers of the era. Elastic Email grew organically without significant external venture funding, building a dual-office structure with operations in Vancouver, Canada and Szczecin, Poland, which has remained its operating model for over a decade. Unlike several of its larger competitors, Elastic Email has not been acquired and continues to operate as an independent, privately held company. Over the years it has expanded from a pure SMTP relay into a fuller marketing suite, adding features like list validation, suppression list management, dedicated IP address options, and, more recently, AI-assisted tools for generating subject lines and campaign content as email marketers increasingly look to automate creative work.

Technically, Elastic Email routes outbound mail through a shared or dedicated sending infrastructure that manages IP warm-up, sender reputation, and feedback loops with major mailbox providers such as Gmail, Outlook, and Yahoo. Developers integrate primarily through a REST API or an SMTP relay endpoint, both of which support features like custom headers, templated merge fields, attachments, and webhook-based event tracking for opens, clicks, bounces, and complaints. The platform includes built-in domain authentication support for SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, along with tools to monitor sender reputation and inbox placement. For marketing sends, users build campaigns with a visual drag-and-drop editor, manage contact lists with custom fields and segmentation, and configure automation workflows triggered by subscriber behavior, all of which report back into the same analytics dashboard used for transactional traffic.

Elastic Email's pricing follows a hybrid model of pay-as-you-go credits and monthly subscription tiers, split across two product lines. The Email API line starts with a free plan offering roughly 3,000 emails per month to about 1,000 contacts, useful for testing integrations before committing to a paid tier. The Starter plan is priced at $19 per month and includes up to 50,000 emails monthly with tracking and analytics, while the Pro plan at $49 per month adds inbound email processing, webhooks, reseller capabilities, sub-account management, and custom reverse DNS for dedicated sending domains. Volume-based tiers scale up from there, covering ranges from 50,000 emails per month up to 10 million emails per month with corresponding price increases. The separate Email Marketing product line starts around $29 per month for smaller contact lists, such as up to 2,500 contacts, and scales based on the number of contacts stored rather than emails sent. Add-ons include dedicated private IP addresses, priced separately per month, and premium support tiers for customers who need faster response times or deliverability consulting.

The platform's target audience skews toward small and mid-sized businesses, SaaS companies, and individual developers who need reliable, affordable transactional email without the enterprise sales process and pricing of larger providers. Its self-serve signup flow, transparent pricing calculator, and combined API-plus-marketing feature set make it particularly attractive to startups building a product that needs both system emails and periodic newsletters from the same account. Agencies and resellers are also a meaningful segment, since Elastic Email's Pro plan explicitly supports white-label reseller functionality and sub-account management for managing multiple clients under one master account.

In the competitive landscape, Elastic Email sits alongside SendGrid, Mailgun, Postmark, and MailerSend as one of the more developer-oriented transactional email providers, but it differentiates itself primarily on price and on bundling marketing features that some competitors sell separately or not at all. SendGrid offers a broadly similar free tier and strong deliverability reputation but has historically priced its higher-volume plans more aggressively than Elastic Email. Postmark focuses almost exclusively on transactional email with a reputation for exceptionally fast, reliable delivery, deliberately avoiding bulk marketing traffic to protect its sender reputation, which makes it a poor fit for customers wanting a single vendor for both use cases. Mailgun, owned by Sinch, targets a similar developer audience with a comparable free tier and API-first design. Elastic Email's pitch against all three is that a single account and a single reputation pool can handle both transactional and promotional mail at a lower blended cost, which appeals to cost-sensitive teams willing to manage their own deliverability rather than paying for a specialist provider's premium.

Elastic Email has reported roughly $6.8 million in annual revenue and around 30,000 customers as of the mid-2020s, figures that reflect a profitable, self-funded growth trajectory rather than the venture-backed scaling pattern of larger rivals. The company has never disclosed a funding round, and public data suggests it has grown without institutional investment, which is notable in a category where most competitors have raised tens or hundreds of millions of dollars. This bootstrapped history shapes its product decisions: pricing tends to undercut venture-backed rivals, and the product roadmap has prioritized practical deliverability and cost-efficiency features over flashy enterprise add-ons.

Architecturally, Elastic Email operates a multi-tenant sending infrastructure with shared and dedicated IP pool options, automated bounce and complaint handling, and real-time event webhooks that feed into both its own analytics dashboard and third-party systems via API. The platform supports standard authentication protocols for email deliverability and provides tools for monitoring domain and IP reputation over time, which is critical for high-volume senders trying to maintain inbox placement rather than being routed to spam folders. Its API documentation covers common integration patterns for popular programming languages and frameworks, and the SMTP relay option allows drop-in replacement for existing mail server configurations without code changes, which lowers the switching cost for developers migrating off self-hosted mail servers or other providers.

In terms of market position, Elastic Email occupies a value-oriented niche within a crowded transactional and marketing email category dominated by well-funded players like SendGrid (Twilio), Mailgun (Sinch), and marketing-first platforms like Mailchimp and Klaviyo. It does not attempt to compete on brand recognition or enterprise sales motion, instead relying on word-of-mouth among developers and cost-conscious small businesses, competitive pricing calculators, and a straightforward self-serve signup experience. Its continued independence and profitability after more than a decade in a category with heavy consolidation and acquisition activity is itself a notable data point about the durability of its niche positioning. As email deliverability becomes more complex due to stricter sender requirements from major mailbox providers, Elastic Email has continued to invest in reputation management tooling and, more recently, AI-assisted content generation features aimed at helping smaller teams produce marketing campaigns without dedicated copywriting resources.

It lets a single account send both system-triggered messages, like password resets and receipts, and bulk marketing campaigns through the same sending infrastructure and reputation pool.

Founded in 2010 by Polish and Canadian engineers, the company has grown as an independent, self-funded business with offices in Vancouver, Canada and Szczecin, Poland, serving roughly 30,000 customers worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features

Deliverability tooling covers SPF, DKIM, and DMARC domain authentication, dedicated IP address options, sender reputation monitoring, and suppression list management, all aimed at helping senders maintain inbox placement at scale.

Add-ons include dedicated private IP addresses and premium support, and pricing is generally positioned below comparable developer-focused competitors like SendGrid, Mailgun, and Postmark.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Elasticsearch is an open-source, distributed search and analytics engine built on top of Apache Lucene, designed to store, search, and analyze large volumes of structured and unstructured data in near real time. It exposes a JSON-based REST API for indexing documents and running complex queries, ranging from simple full-text search to aggregations, geospatial queries, and vector similarity search for AI and machine learning workloads. Elasticsearch is the foundational component of the broader Elastic Stack, commonly known by its earlier acronym ELK, which pairs Elasticsearch with Logstash for data ingestion and transformation and Kibana for visualization and dashboarding, later extended with Beats for lightweight data shippers. Today Elastic markets the combined platform around three primary solution areas: Elasticsearch as a general-purpose search and vector database, Elastic Observability for logs, metrics, and application performance monitoring, and Elastic Security for SIEM and endpoint protection.

The project's origins trace back to Shay Banon, an Israeli developer who in 2004 built a full-text search server called Compass on top of Apache Lucene as a way to add search to his wife's cooking-recipe application. Compass proved difficult to scale, so Banon rewrote it from the ground up as a distributed system and released the first version of Elasticsearch in February 2010. The project gained rapid adoption due to its schema-flexible JSON document model, horizontal scalability, and simple REST API, and by 2012 Banon co-founded the company Elasticsearch BV, later renamed Elastic N.V., together with Uri Boness, Simon Willnauer, and Steven Schuurman, all of whom had backgrounds in search and Apache Lucene development. In 2015 the company acquired Found, a hosted Elasticsearch provider, which became the foundation of what is now Elastic Cloud, and shortly afterward the company rebranded from Elasticsearch to Elastic to reflect its broadening product suite beyond the core search engine. Elastic went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker ESTC in October 2018, and the company has since grown into a Dutch-American multinational headquartered in Amsterdam with a large US presence in Mountain View, California, employing several thousand people worldwide.

Licensing has been one of the most consequential and closely watched aspects of Elasticsearch's history. The software was originally released under the permissive Apache License 2.0, which allowed anyone, including cloud providers, to freely host and resell it. In January 2021, Elastic announced that starting with version 7.11, Elasticsearch and Kibana would move to a dual license combining the Server Side Public License and the Elastic License, neither of which the Open Source Initiative recognizes as open source. Elastic framed the change as a response to Amazon Web Services offering a managed Elasticsearch service that Elastic felt free-rode on its investment without adequate collaboration or revenue sharing; AWS responded by forking the last Apache-licensed version into what became OpenSearch, now stewarded under the Linux Foundation. The relicensing was controversial within the open-source community and drew criticism for restricting how organizations could deploy and resell the software. In August 2024, in a notable reversal, Elastic added the GNU Affero General Public License, or AGPL, as a third licensing option for Elasticsearch and Kibana starting with version 8.16.0, effectively making the software freely available under a recognized open-source license once again, alongside the two source-available options that remained in place for customers who prefer them. Elastic's CTO cited a changed competitive and cloud landscape as the reason for the reversal.

Technically, Elasticsearch operates as a distributed cluster of nodes, each capable of storing and indexing data using inverted indexes derived from Apache Lucene's segment-based storage model. Data is organized into indices, which are split into shards distributed across nodes for horizontal scalability, with replica shards providing redundancy and fault tolerance. Clients interact with the cluster through a RESTful HTTP API using JSON, supporting operations such as document indexing, full-text search with relevance scoring, structured filtering, aggregations for analytics, geospatial queries, and, in more recent versions, dense vector fields and approximate nearest-neighbor search used for semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, and other AI and machine learning applications. Elastic has invested heavily in this vector search and generative AI integration layer, positioning Elasticsearch as a vector database option for enterprises building AI applications, alongside its long-standing strengths in log analytics and enterprise search.

Pricing for Elasticsearch follows a dual structure. The self-managed, open-source-licensed software is free to download, deploy, and operate on a customer's own infrastructure, with Elastic monetizing through paid subscription tiers that unlock proprietary features like advanced security, machine learning, and enterprise support, available for both self-managed and Elastic Cloud deployments. Elastic Cloud, the company's managed hosting offering, is priced through resource-based tiers historically named Standard, Gold, Platinum, and Enterprise, with baseline starting prices in the range of roughly $95 to $99 per month for Standard, rising through Gold and Platinum to Enterprise plans starting around $184 per month for minimal deployments; actual production costs scale substantially with data volume, node size, and retention, with modest production clusters commonly running from roughly $1,500 to $8,000 per month and larger, security-focused deployments running tens of thousands of dollars per month. Elastic has also introduced serverless, consumption-based pricing options that charge per gigabyte ingested and stored rather than by fixed node-hour pricing, aimed at simplifying cost management for variable workloads.

Elasticsearch's user base spans a wide spectrum from individual developers running a local instance for a side project to some of the largest enterprises in the world using it for mission-critical search, observability, and security infrastructure. Typical users include platform and backend engineers building full-text search into applications, DevOps and site reliability teams using the ELK stack for centralized log aggregation and monitoring, and security operations teams using Elastic Security as a SIEM platform. Its flexibility and broad ecosystem of client libraries, covering virtually every major programming language, along with a large community of official and community-maintained plugins and integrations, have made it a default choice for search and log analytics infrastructure across industries ranging from e-commerce to financial services to government.

Within the competitive landscape, Elasticsearch's most direct rival is OpenSearch, the AWS-led fork created in response to the 2021 relicensing, which remains under the Apache 2.0 license and is developed under Linux Foundation governance with backing from AWS, among others. Apache Solr, another Lucene-based search engine, predates Elasticsearch and remains a viable alternative for some search-only workloads but has lost significant market share to Elasticsearch's more modern distributed architecture and richer ecosystem. In the hosted and managed search-as-a-service category, Algolia targets developers wanting an easier-to-integrate, purpose-built search API without operating a cluster, while newer entrants like Meilisearch and Typesense compete on developer experience and simplicity for smaller-scale search use cases. In the log analytics and observability space, Elasticsearch and Kibana compete with Splunk, Datadog, and Grafana Loki, with Elastic frequently positioning its combined Elasticsearch-plus-observability offering as a lower-cost alternative to Splunk's consumption-based licensing model.

Elasticsearch's market position remains strong more than fifteen years after its initial release, underpinned by its status as one of the most widely deployed search and analytics engines in production use, a large enterprise customer base, and continued heavy investment in AI and vector search capabilities that keep it relevant as organizations build retrieval-augmented generation and semantic search applications. The 2024 return to AGPL licensing was widely interpreted as an acknowledgment that the open-source ecosystem, developer goodwill, and competitive pressure from OpenSearch mattered more to Elastic's long-term growth than the short-term protection the 2021 relicensing was intended to provide, and it reflects a broader industry pattern of infrastructure companies recalibrating open-source strategy as cloud competition and community sentiment evolve.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Elasticsearch is a distributed search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene, offering a JSON REST API for full-text search, structured queries, aggregations, and vector similarity search. It forms the core of the Elastic Stack, alongside Logstash for data ingestion and Kibana for visualization.

Originally released in February 2010 by Shay Banon as a successor to his earlier Compass search server, Elasticsearch became the foundation of Elastic N.V., a company founded in 2012 that went public on the NYSE under the ticker ESTC in 2018.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include horizontally scalable, sharded indices, near real-time full-text search with relevance scoring, powerful aggregation queries for analytics, geospatial search, and dense vector fields for semantic and AI-driven search.

The broader Elastic Stack adds Kibana for dashboards and visualization, Logstash and Beats for data ingestion, and dedicated solution layers for observability (logs, metrics, APM) and security (SIEM, endpoint protection).

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Elasticsearch is free and open source to self-host, currently offered under a choice of the Elastic License, the Server Side Public License, or, since August 2024, the AGPL, after a widely discussed 2021 relicensing away from Apache 2.0 that prompted AWS to create the competing OpenSearch fork.

Elastic monetizes primarily through Elastic Cloud, its managed hosting service, with resource-based tiers historically named Standard, Gold, Platinum, and Enterprise, plus newer serverless, consumption-based pricing options billed per gigabyte ingested and stored.

faqs.question: Is Elasticsearch free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Elasticsearch can be self-hosted for free under a choice of licenses, including the AGPL open-source license reintroduced in August 2024; Elastic monetizes primarily through paid Elastic Cloud hosting and premium feature tiers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Elasticsearch and the ELK stack?
faqs.answer: Elasticsearch is the search and analytics engine itself, while the ELK stack refers to Elasticsearch combined with Logstash for data ingestion and Kibana for visualization, often extended with Beats for lightweight data shipping.

faqs.question: Why did Elasticsearch change its license in 2021?
faqs.answer: Elastic moved Elasticsearch and Kibana from Apache 2.0 to a dual Server Side Public License and Elastic License in January 2021, citing concerns that cloud providers like AWS were offering the software as a service without adequate collaboration or revenue sharing.

faqs.question: Did Elasticsearch become open source again?
faqs.answer: Yes, in August 2024 Elastic added the AGPL as a third licensing option starting with version 8.16.0, making Elasticsearch and Kibana available under a recognized open-source license again alongside the existing source-available options.

faqs.question: What is the main open-source alternative to Elasticsearch?
faqs.answer: OpenSearch, an Apache 2.0-licensed fork created by AWS and now governed under the Linux Foundation, is the most direct open-source alternative that emerged in response to Elastic's 2021 relicensing.

faqs.question: Who created Elasticsearch?
faqs.answer: Shay Banon created Elasticsearch, releasing the first version in February 2010 as a distributed successor to his earlier Compass search server; he later co-founded Elastic N.V. in 2012 with Uri Boness, Simon Willnauer, and Steven Schuurman.

enrichment.short_description: Element is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted messaging and collaboration app built on the Matrix protocol, offering decentralized, self-hostable team chat as a secure alternative to Slack, Teams, and Discord.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source / Freemium (free self-hosted Community edition plus paid Enterprise and Sovereign tiers)

enrichment.seo_title: Element Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Element review 2026: Matrix protocol messaging, end-to-end encryption, self-hosting with Synapse, pricing tiers, and how it compares to Slack and Signal.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Element is an open-source instant messaging and team collaboration application built on top of Matrix, an open standard and protocol for decentralized, end-to-end encrypted real-time communication. Rather than operating as a single centralized service like Slack or Microsoft Teams, Element functions as a client that can connect to any Matrix-compatible server, known as a homeserver, allowing organizations to self-host their entire communication infrastructure or rely on independently operated public and private servers that federate with one another, similar in spirit to how email servers interoperate across providers. Element supports one-to-one and group messaging, voice and video calls, file sharing, threads, and integrations with external tools, all wrapped in end-to-end encryption by default for private rooms, making it positioned as a security- and sovereignty-focused alternative to mainstream proprietary chat platforms.

The roots of both Matrix and Element trace back to Matthew Hodgson and Amandine Le Pape, who began developing the Matrix protocol as a side project while working at Amdocs, aiming to create what they described as a missing decentralized communication layer for the open web. In 2015, Amdocs formalized this work by creating a subsidiary called Vector Creations Limited to house the Matrix team, and the first client application, released in 2016, was originally named Vector before being renamed Riot later that same year. In July 2017, Amdocs withdrew its funding, and the core team spun out to form their own independent, UK-based company, New Vector Limited, to continue developing Matrix and the Riot client without corporate backing. In October 2018, the non-profit Matrix.org Foundation was established as a neutral, UK-based Community Interest Company to govern the open Matrix standard independently of any single commercial entity, ensuring the protocol itself would not be controlled by one company even as New Vector continued to fund the majority of its core development. In mid-2019, the Riot client and the company itself were rebranded to Element, the name under which the product is known today, and the company later restructured its branding to Element as well, operating through regional entities including Element Creations Limited in the UK, alongside subsidiaries in Germany, France, and the United States.

Element has raised approximately $43.5 million in total venture funding over its history, including an $8.5 million round in October 2019 and a $30 million Series B round in July 2021, funds it has used to accelerate both product development and continued investment in the underlying Matrix protocol, since Element has historically funded the majority of core Matrix development directly. The company has built a notable public-sector customer base, reflecting Matrix and Element's appeal to governments and security-conscious organizations that want data sovereignty and cannot rely on foreign-hosted proprietary platforms; examples include the French government's Tchap messenger, built on Matrix in partnership with New Vector, and BwMessenger, a Matrix-based secure messaging app built for the German Armed Forces (Bundeswehr) in partnership with the German IT services firm BWI. Element and Matrix have also seen adoption in other government and regulated environments in the UK, France, Germany, Ukraine, and elsewhere, positioning the ecosystem as a serious alternative to Slack, Microsoft Teams, and Discord for organizations with strict sovereignty or security requirements.

Technically, Element's security model rests on the Matrix protocol's implementation of the Olm and Megolm cryptographic ratchets, derived from the Double Ratchet Algorithm popularized by Signal, providing end-to-end encryption for one-to-one and group conversations, including cross-signed device verification to protect against impersonation and man-in-the-middle attacks. Because Matrix is a federated protocol, messages are synchronized across homeservers using a shared, append-only event graph, allowing users on different servers to communicate seamlessly while each server retains its own copy of relevant conversation history, giving organizations control over data residency and retention rather than depending on a single vendor's infrastructure. The reference homeserver implementation is Synapse, written in Python and maintained by Element, though the Matrix ecosystem also includes alternative homeserver implementations such as Dendrite, a lighter-weight Go implementation intended for smaller deployments and better performance characteristics, along with community-built implementations like Conduit. Element ships client applications for web, desktop (Windows, macOS, and Linux), and mobile (iOS and Android), all built against the same open Matrix Client-Server API, and it also offers Element Call, a native video and voice conferencing feature integrated directly into the app using WebRTC.

Element's licensing and pricing model reflects its open-source foundation. The Element client and the reference Synapse homeserver are released under the GNU Affero General Public License, meaning the full feature set is available for free to any organization willing to self-host its own Matrix infrastructure, with no artificial caps on users, messages, or core functionality, unlike many freemium SaaS products that gate features behind paywalls. Element's commercial pricing is built around three current tiers: a free Community tier intended for self-hosted deployments of up to about 100 users, running the Synapse Community edition homeserver and including Element Call and the full open-source frontend and backend, at no cost. An Enterprise tier, available either as a fully managed Element Enterprise Cloud offering or as an on-premise deployment starting from roughly 100 users, adds the more scalable Synapse Pro homeserver, enhanced security and support, LDAP and Active Directory or SSO authentication, data retention and export policies, long-term support versions, Kubernetes-based Helm chart deployment, and service-level agreements with defined response times, priced per seat per month and available on request. A Sovereign tier extends the Enterprise feature set to fully air-gapped deployments with no internet connectivity required, aimed at defense, intelligence, and other high-security government environments that need isolated, high-side infrastructure, also priced on a custom, per-deployment basis. Historically, Element also offered Element Matrix Services, or EMS, a hosted Matrix infrastructure product for developers, with previously published per-monthly-active-user pricing around $3 for a Standard plan and $4 for an Enterprise plan, though the company has since simplified its public pricing page around the current three-tier Community, Enterprise, and Sovereign structure.

Element's target audience spans a wide range, from individual privacy-conscious users and open-source communities who self-host a small Synapse instance for personal or group use, to mid-sized organizations wanting a Slack alternative they fully control, up to national governments and defense agencies requiring air-gapped, sovereign messaging infrastructure that cannot depend on any foreign commercial cloud provider. This breadth is unusual among team chat platforms, most of which target either consumers or businesses but rarely both extremes of the security spectrum simultaneously.

In the competitive landscape, Element and Matrix compete on different axes depending on the segment. Against mainstream centralized team chat platforms like Slack, Microsoft Teams, and Discord, Element's primary differentiation is decentralization, self-hosting, and data sovereignty, appealing to organizations wary of vendor lock-in or foreign data jurisdiction. Against other secure messaging apps like Signal, Element differentiates on its federated, multi-server architecture and richer collaboration feature set aimed at organizations and teams rather than Signal's centralized, consumer-focused, one-to-one and small-group messaging model. Within the Matrix ecosystem itself, Element is the dominant and best-resourced client, though alternative Matrix clients exist, such as FluffyChat, Cinny, and SchildiChat, built by community developers on top of the same open protocol, illustrating one of Matrix's core design goals: that no single company, including Element, controls the network or protocol itself.

Element's market position has been strengthened by its early and sustained relationships with public-sector and government customers across Europe, which has given it a credibility advantage in security-sensitive procurement processes that few consumer-oriented chat platforms can match. Its open governance model, through the independent Matrix.org Foundation, also mitigates a common concern in developer and enterprise software adoption: dependency on a single vendor's continued existence or business decisions, since the underlying protocol and reference server implementations remain open source and forkable even if Element the company were to change direction. As of 2026, ongoing coverage highlights growing adoption of Matrix-based secure messaging within government IT departments internationally, reflecting continued momentum for the sovereign, self-hosted communication category that Element and Matrix pioneered.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Element is an open-source messaging and collaboration client built on the Matrix protocol, a decentralized, federated standard for real-time, end-to-end encrypted communication. Users can self-host their own Matrix homeserver or rely on public and private servers that interoperate with one another.

Originally launched as Vector in 2016 and renamed Riot later that year, the app and its parent company were rebranded to Element in 2019. The company, New Vector Limited, spun out of Amdocs subsidiary Vector Creations Limited in 2017 and is now headquartered in Chiswick, London.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Element supports one-to-one and group messaging, voice and video calls via Element Call, file sharing, threads, and end-to-end encryption by default using the Olm and Megolm cryptographic ratchets derived from the Double Ratchet Algorithm.

Clients are available for web, desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux), and mobile (iOS, Android), all built on the open Matrix Client-Server API, and the platform can be self-hosted using the Synapse or Dendrite homeserver implementations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Element's Community tier is free and fully open source under the AGPL, supporting self-hosted deployments of up to about 100 users with no artificial feature limits.

Paid Enterprise and Sovereign tiers, priced per seat per month on request, add managed hosting or on-premise deployment at scale, enhanced security, SSO and directory integration, SLAs, and, for Sovereign, fully air-gapped operation for government and defense use cases.

pros: Fully open source under the AGPL with no artificial caps on users, messages, or core features, even on the free self-hosted tier.
Decentralized, federated architecture gives organizations full control over data residency, unlike single-vendor centralized platforms.
End-to-end encryption is enabled by default in private rooms, backed by a well-regarded cryptographic protocol design.
Strong track record with government and defense customers, including deployments in France and Germany, validating its security posture.
cons: Self-hosting a Synapse homeserver requires meaningful technical expertise and ongoing infrastructure maintenance.
Enterprise and Sovereign tier pricing is not published and requires contacting sales, complicating budget comparisons against competitors.
The federated architecture and richer feature set can feel more complex than simpler, purely consumer-focused apps like Signal.
Some advanced enterprise features, such as SLAs and directory integration, are reserved for paid tiers rather than the free Community edition.

faqs.question: Is Element free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Element's Community edition is free and open source under the AGPL, supporting self-hosted deployments of up to about 100 users with the full feature set and no artificial limits.

faqs.question: What is the Matrix protocol and how does it relate to Element?
faqs.answer: Matrix is an open standard for decentralized, end-to-end encrypted real-time communication; Element is the flagship client and primary commercial sponsor built on top of it, though anyone can build alternative Matrix clients or homeservers.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Element?
faqs.answer: Yes, organizations can self-host their own Matrix homeserver using Synapse, the reference implementation maintained by Element, or Dendrite, a lighter-weight alternative, giving full control over data storage and retention.

faqs.question: Was Element previously called Riot or Vector?
faqs.answer: Yes, the app launched as Vector in 2016, was renamed Riot later that year, and was rebranded to Element in 2019 along with the parent company.

faqs.question: Does Element support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, Element enables end-to-end encryption by default in private rooms using the Olm and Megolm cryptographic ratchets, based on the Double Ratchet Algorithm also used by Signal.

faqs.question: How much does Element Enterprise cost?
faqs.answer: Element's Enterprise and Sovereign tiers are priced per seat per month on a custom basis available on request; the company has previously published reference pricing around $3 to $4 per monthly active user for its Element Matrix Services hosting product.
